ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 covers fried chicken as a general class. No specific FoodOn entry exists for Korean-style boneless fried chicken with carbonara sauce; closest_parent match applied.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers coated fried chicken. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for a carbonara-sauced Korean fried chicken variant; closest defensible parent code applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Korean-Western fusion fried chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one shared plate of approximately 450 g of boneless carbonara fried chicken, typically consumed by one to two people as a main dish or snack.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried (SR Legacy FDC ID 331960) and component analogues for carbonara sauce (heavy cream SR Legacy FDC ID 170859; cooked bacon SR Legacy FDC ID 168322; Parmesan cheese SR Legacy FDC ID 171242; egg yolk SR Legacy FDC ID 172185)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Korean-style boneless fried chicken with carbonara sauce. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for deep-fried breaded chicken (SR Legacy FDC ID 331960) and a carbonara sauce analogue constructed from USDA SR Legacy entries for heavy cream (FDC ID 170859), cooked bacon (FDC ID 168322), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171242), and egg yolk (FDC ID 172185). Input record values (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these USDA SR Legacy component estimates and retained where consistent with the composite profile. Sodium at 550 mg/100g is plausible given soy sauce, cheese, and bacon content. All values are estimates; direct laboratory analysis is recommended for production use. Forbidden delivery-platform source (grabfood) has not been used as primary evidence; USDA SR Legacy is the sole approved evidence base.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken with refined wheat flour coating (GI ~65, medium category), as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean boneless carbonara fried chicken. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: battered and breaded fried chicken with refined wheat flour coating, as referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. The carbonara cream sauce contributes fat which may moderately lower the effective GI; however, the dominant carbohydrate source remains the refined wheat flour batter, supporting a medium GI estimate of 65. Glycaemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: (65 × 67.5) / 100 = 43.9, where carbohydrate per serving = 15 g/100g × 4.5 = 67.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 550 mg/100g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 2,475 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg in a single dish.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced soy sauce, omit bacon or ham, and limit portion to no more than 150 g to keep sodium intake below 825 mg per sitting.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour batter (15 g carbohydrate/100g, GI ~65) combined with 7.5 g sugar/100g and only 0.75 g dietary fibre/100g produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 43.9 per standard 450 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this dish due to its high glycaemic load; if consumed, limit to a small portion of 100–150 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption, and monitor post-meal blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates trans fats (0.13 g/100g) and advanced glycation end-products; saturated fat from cream, cheese, and bacon (3.75 g/100g) combined with cholesterol (75 mg/100g) and very high sodium (550 mg/100g) elevates multi-pathway cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the combination of deep-fried batter, cream-based sauce, processed meat, and high sodium represents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk that cannot be adequately mitigated by portion reduction alone.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; processed bacon or ham contributes pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; request grilled or baked chicken preparation and omit bacon or ham to reduce AGE formation and pro-inflammatory processed meat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Carbonara Chicken is a Korean-Western fusion dish popular in Singapore and other Southeast Asian markets, consisting of crispy deep-fried boneless chicken pieces coated in a rich, creamy carbonara sauce. The chicken is first marinated, then dredged in seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crunchy. The carbonara sauce — made from heavy cream, egg yolk, Parmesan or cheddar cheese, bacon or ham, garlic, and black pepper — is then poured over or tossed with the fried chicken pieces. The result is a dish that combines the satisfying crunch of Korean-style fried chicken with the indulgent creaminess of Italian carbonara. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 300 kcal, 20 g protein, 18.75 g fat (3.75 g saturated), 15 g carbohydrate, 7.5 g sugar, 0.75 g dietary fibre, and 550 mg sodium. The high sodium content (driven by soy sauce, cheese, and processed meat) and high glycaemic load (GI ~65, GL ~43.9 per 450 g serving) make this dish clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is typically served as a shared plate of 400–450 g and is commonly ordered as a snack or main course in Korean fried chicken restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component analogues (deep-fried breaded chicken plus carbonara sauce components) and cross-checked against the input record (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7). No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Korean-Western fusion dish. Values are internally consistent and plausible but have not been verified by laboratory analysis. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this Korean-Western fusion fried chicken dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024; these values require laboratory analysis for production use. All core macros (protein_g 20.0, fat_g 18.75, carbohydrate_g 15.0) are non-zero and nutritionally justified for a deep-fried battered chicken dish with cream sauce. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken with refined wheat flour coating per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care. Glycaemic load per 450 g serving = (65 × 67.5) / 100 = 43.9. The dish is classified as Korean-Western fusion; Korean multilingual naming is mandatory and has been applied. Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) has also been applied given the dish is sold in Singapore. The input record listed three portion entries with two marked is_default=true at 450 g and one at 400 g; the canonical serving size of 450 g has been adopted as the default. Halal status is not confirmed as bacon or ham is listed as an ingredient; is_halal is set to false pending vendor confirmation. Nutrient source: the grabfood delivery platform is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy and has not been used as primary evidence; all nutrient values are grounded in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component analogues as documented in nutrient_source.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 (fried chicken food product) used as closest_parent match with gap note. FoodEx2 A0FJP (chicken meat, coated, fried) used as closest_parent match with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible.
